title: Roast pork loin with crackling
subtitle: Dry-brined rind, roasted to a brittle snap
excerpt: Pork loin with the rind scored and dried for cracking crackling, roasted at high heat then lower to finish through. The secret is dry skin, salt draws out moisture, the blast phase blisters the rind, and the result should shatter. Served with apple sauce and roast potatoes.
sourceNotes: Pork crackling is one of the most sought-after elements of the British Sunday roast. The rind must be completely dry and deeply scored before it goes into the oven. Many failed cracklings come from a damp rind or insufficient scoring. The dry-salt method overnight is the most reliable approach.

content.text: The night before: score the rind in lines 5 mm apart, cutting through the fat but not into the meat. Pat completely dry with kitchen paper. Rub fine salt into the scored lines and all over the surface. Place uncovered on a rack in the fridge. By morning the rind will be visibly drier and white with drawn-out salt.

Ask your butcher to score the rind if you are unsure. The scoring must go right through the fat layer, insufficient depth is the most common reason crackling does not blister properly. A sharp Stanley knife works better than a kitchen knife for this.

items.name: Pork loin
items.prepNote: rind on, scored deeply in 5 mm lines

items.name: Fine sea salt
items.prepNote: rubbed into the rind the night before

items.name: Black pepper
items.prepNote: coarsely ground, rubbed into the flesh only

items.name: Vegetable oil
items.prepNote: rubbed into the flesh only, not the rind

content.text: Remove the {{pork-loin}} from the fridge 1 hour before cooking. Pat the rind completely dry with kitchen paper, any moisture remaining will steam instead of blister. Rub the flesh (not the rind) with {{vegetable-oil}}, {{black-pepper}}, and {{fennel-seeds}} if using. Place rind-side up in a roasting tin. Heat the oven to 240°C. Roast at 240°C for 25 minutes: the rind should blister and bubble dramatically. Reduce to 180°C and roast for a further 50 to 60 minutes, until a thermometer reads 70°C in the thickest part. Rest on a warm board for 20 minutes. The crackling will continue to crisp as it rests. Carve by cutting through the crackling with a sharp knife or breaking it into pieces first.

items.fix: Dry overnight in the fridge is essential. If you forgot, pat very dry and blast under a hot grill for 5 minutes after roasting, watch it closely
items.cause: Rind too moist going into the oven
items.symptom: Crackling soft and chewy rather than brittle

items.fix: The scored lines must be 5 mm apart and reach through all the fat. After the blast phase, if areas are still flat, briefly remove the meat, score those areas deeper, and return to the oven
items.cause: Rind not scored evenly, or scored too shallowly
items.symptom: Rind bubbled but not fully blistered across the whole surface

items.fix: A thermometer reading of 70°C in the thickest part is correct, remove at 68°C and let carry-over cooking do the rest. Small joints cook faster and need shorter times at the reduced temperature
items.cause: Oven too hot for too long, or joint too small
items.symptom: Meat dry and overcooked under properly done crackling

content.text: Pork crackling is a test of the cook in a way that most other roasts are not. It either works or it doesn't, and the room knows immediately. A successful crackling has a physical quality that people remember: the brittle snap, the hot fat underneath. The loin is the most consistent cut for crackling; the belly produces more of it but requires different treatment.
